narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 51% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to flag higher-risk applicants. This prediction is based on your academic profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.20—which is very close to the typical student's 3.21—your LSAT score of 29, well below the average of about 37, and your rankings in the bottom decile for both first-year and third-year school grades, compared to mid-level averages around the fifth or sixth decile for most students. Your background as a white male from an upper-middle-income family planning to attend full-time aligns closely with the majority of applicants, who are predominantly full-time white males from similar income brackets. When the model weighed all this, your third-year grade decile had the strongest influence, pushing the prediction toward expecting a bar failure because it's in the lowest group. Right behind that was your first-year decile, also at the bottom, which similarly nudged the odds higher for failure. Your LSAT score came next in influence, with its lower-than-average mark contributing in the same direction toward the riskier outcome. Family income level, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status followed in descending order of impact, each adding a bit more toward the failure prediction, though less forcefully. Overall, your early and later school rankings and LSAT stood out as the biggest drags compared to peers who typically pass the bar. The good news is that several of these factors are within your control for the future. To boost your bar-passing odds, focus on improving your grades to climb out of the bottom deciles, perhaps by seeking tutoring or study strategies early on. Retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above the average could make a big difference, as it's a key influencer. Your GPA is already near average, so maintaining or slightly raising it while building stronger performance in law school will help shift future predictions in your favor. Keep pursuing your goal—you're close in some areas, and targeted improvements can turn this around.
